title: Mastitis & Breastfeeding Issues
oneLiner: Inflammation of breast tissue, usually in lactating women; requires prompt antibiotics and continued breastfeeding to prevent abscess and septic complications.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Septic shock due to bacterial mastitis (Staph aureus, Strep spp.):** high fever (>39°C) + rigors + tachycardia/hypotension + leukocytosis → treat with IV antibiotics and fluid resuscitation immediately; delay can lead to multi-organ failure.
- **Breast abscess (undrained):** fluctuant, erythematous, tender mass that fails to improve after 48 h of antibiotics; single finding = palpable fluctuance on exam or USS-confirmed collection → needs aspiration or incision & drainage within 24 h to avoid extension/fistula.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Unilateral breast pain, erythema, firm swelling (often wedge-shaped)
  - Fever, myalgia, malaise (systemic infection)
  - Palpable tender lump (may indicate blocked duct vs. abscess)
  - Nipple fissure or pain (portal of entry for bacteria)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Erythema, warmth, peau d'orange (rarely – think inflammatory breast cancer)
  - Fluctuance (abscess)
  - Swollen axillary lymph nodes (ipsilateral)
  - Mastitis is usually unilateral; bilateral engorgement suggests non-infective cause
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:** Infective mastitis (localised, tender, with fever) vs. **breast engorgement** (bilateral, diffuse, without fever, relieved by feeding)
- **Less likely:**
  - Blocked duct / milk stasis (no fever, resolves with feeding/expression)
  - Inflammatory breast cancer (non-lactating, peau d’orange, no fever, fixed mass)
  - Galactocoele (painless cyst after weaning)
  - Cellulitis (rarely in breastfeeding)
- **Splitter:** Presence of fever >38°C and systemic symptoms → infective mastitis; no fever → blocked duct or engorgement.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Clinical breast exam – assess for fluctuance
  - Check temperature, BP, HR, RR, SpO₂ → NEWS2
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (WBC may be elevated or normal)
  - CRP (elevated in infection)
  - Blood cultures if septic (two sets)
  - Lactate if NEWS2 ≥5 or hypotensive
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Breast USS – if abscess suspected (palpable fluctuance or failure to improve after 48 h of antibiotics)
  - **Second-line:** Mammogram / MRI only if non-lactating or suspicion of cancer
- **Special tests:**
  - Milk culture (from expressed breast milk or nipple swab) – only for recurrent or treatment-resistant mastitis
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis – no scoring system required
- **Never-miss test:** **Breast USS** when abscess is suspected – omission leads to delayed drainage and ongoing sepsis.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Start empiric antibiotics if fever or systemic symptoms present (see Drugs & Doses)
  - Advise to continue breastfeeding or expressing from both breasts (including affected side) – do not stop feeding
  - Warm compress or gentle massage before feeds to promote outflow
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g QDS or ibuprofen 400mg TDS (if no contraindications)
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - If admitted (sepsis, abscess, high NEWS2): IV antibiotics, IV fluids (0.9% sodium chloride 500ml–1L stat, then [check local protocol] for rate)
  - If abscess confirmed: arrange aspiration (USS-guided) or incision & drainage – refer to breast surgery team
  - Monitor temperature, pain, and resolution of erythema
  - Support lactation: lactation consultant referral
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete 10–14 day course of antibiotics (oral switch when afebrile for 24 h)
  - Continue breastfeeding on both sides; if infant refuses affected breast, hand express and feed by cup
  - Consider probiotic (Lactobacillus) for recurrent mastitis – limited evidence but low risk
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 125 ml/h (adjust for shock)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (unless low risk or contraindicated)
  - Diet: normal diet, encourage oral intake (no NBM)
  - Observations: 4-hourly (more frequent if septic – 1–2 hourly)
  - Breastfeed/pump as usual (involve midwifery/neonatal team)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Flucloxacillin** 500 mg QDS PO (mild–moderate) OR 1 g QDS IV (severe) for 10–14 days – covers *Staph aureus*.
  - If penicillin allergy (non-anaphylaxis): **Cefalexin** 500 mg QDS PO (or 1 g IV) – cross-reactivity low.
  - If penicillin anaphylaxis: **Clindamycin** 300–450 mg QDS PO (or 600 mg IV QDS) – covers *Staph* and *Strep*.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Co-amoxiclav** 625 mg TDS PO (if cephalosporin allergy or mixed infection) – note: may cause diarrhoea in infant.
  - MRSA risk (known colonisation, recurrent): **Vancomycin** IV (dose per protocol) – seek microbiology advice.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Flucloxacillin + warfarin – enhanced anticoagulation (monitor INR)
  - Clindamycin – higher risk of *C. difficile* infection
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Flucloxacillin: avoid higher doses (e.g. >4 g/day IV) in hepatic impairment – risk of jaundice (especially elderly)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Prescribing flucloxacillin without confirming penicillin allergy status** – can cause anaphylaxis. Always ask: “Have you ever had a reaction to penicillin?”
  - **Omitting analgesia** – pain inhibits milk let-down; ensure paracetamol/ibuprofen prescribed.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Breast abscess (requires drainage)
  - Septic shock
  - Recurrent mastitis (if incomplete treatment or underlying blocked duct)
- **Chronic:**
  - Milk fistula (after incision & drainage – rare)
  - Scarring or fibrosis
  - Inflammatory breast cancer mimic – delayed diagnosis if not considered
sections.Prognosis: - Excellent with early antibiotics – >90% resolve without abscess. Abscess requires drainage but breastfeeding can continue. Recurrence rate ~10–20% if unresolved risk factors (poor latch, nipple damage). No increase in mortality.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always advise continued breastfeeding/expressing** – stopping worsens engorgement and infection risk. If infant refuses, pump and discard (can return to breast after treatment).
- **Consider breast milk culture** for recurrent or antibiotic-resistant cases – send mid-stream milk (after cleaning nipple) to microbiology.
- **Examine both breasts and check for nipple fissures/GDH (group B streptococcus) in pregnancy** – if fissure present, apply mupirocin 2% ointment TDS for 5 days (if crusts/erythema).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT prescribe co-amoxiclav as first-line without checking local guidelines** – may cause diarrhoea in infant; flucloxacillin is preferred.
- **Do NOT delay abscess drainage** – waiting beyond 48 h of medical therapy increases risk of rupture, fistula, and prolonged sepsis.
- **Do NOT dismiss inflammatory breast cancer in a non-lactating woman with persistent erythema** – always refer to urgent breast clinic if no response to 2 weeks of antibiotics.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Fever >38.5°C after 48 h of antibiotics
  - Worsening pain or swelling despite treatment
  - Development of a new, fluctuant lump
  - Inability to breastfeed/express (increasing breast fullness)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review in 48–72 h to assess response
  - If recurrent mastitis (>2 episodes): refer to breast surgeon or lactation consultant
  - If abscess drained: review by breast surgery team in 1–2 weeks
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially respiratory rate >24, SpO2 <92%, systolic BP <90 mmHg)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L despite fluid resuscitation
  - GCS <15 or confusion
  - Abscess not amenable to bedside drainage (requires USS-guided aspiration or I&D in theatre)
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - Septic shock persisting after 1 L fluid bolus
  - Rising lactate (>4 mmol/L)
  - Need for vasopressors or respiratory support

rawMarkdown:
## Mastitis & Breastfeeding Issues
**One-liner:** Inflammation of breast tissue, usually in lactating women; requires prompt antibiotics and continued breastfeeding to prevent abscess and septic complications.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Septic shock due to bacterial mastitis (Staph aureus, Strep spp.):** high fever (>39°C) + rigors + tachycardia/hypotension + leukocytosis → treat with IV antibiotics and fluid resuscitation immediately; delay can lead to multi-organ failure.
- **Breast abscess (undrained):** fluctuant, erythematous, tender mass that fails to improve after 48 h of antibiotics; single finding = palpable fluctuance on exam or USS-confirmed collection → needs aspiration or incision & drainage within 24 h to avoid extension/fistula.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Unilateral breast pain, erythema, firm swelling (often wedge-shaped)
  - Fever, myalgia, malaise (systemic infection)
  - Palpable tender lump (may indicate blocked duct vs. abscess)
  - Nipple fissure or pain (portal of entry for bacteria)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Erythema, warmth, peau d'orange (rarely – think inflammatory breast cancer)
  - Fluctuance (abscess)
  - Swollen axillary lymph nodes (ipsilateral)
  - Mastitis is usually unilateral; bilateral engorgement suggests non-infective cause

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely:** Infective mastitis (localised, tender, with fever) vs. **breast engorgement** (bilateral, diffuse, without fever, relieved by feeding)
- **Less likely:**
  - Blocked duct / milk stasis (no fever, resolves with feeding/expression)
  - Inflammatory breast cancer (non-lactating, peau d’orange, no fever, fixed mass)
  - Galactocoele (painless cyst after weaning)
  - Cellulitis (rarely in breastfeeding)
- **Splitter:** Presence of fever >38°C and systemic symptoms → infective mastitis; no fever → blocked duct or engorgement.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Clinical breast exam – assess for fluctuance
  - Check temperature, BP, HR, RR, SpO₂ → NEWS2
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (WBC may be elevated or normal)
  - CRP (elevated in infection)
  - Blood cultures if septic (two sets)
  - Lactate if NEWS2 ≥5 or hypotensive
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Breast USS – if abscess suspected (palpable fluctuance or failure to improve after 48 h of antibiotics)
  - **Second-line:** Mammogram / MRI only if non-lactating or suspicion of cancer
- **Special tests:**
  - Milk culture (from expressed breast milk or nipple swab) – only for recurrent or treatment-resistant mastitis
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis – no scoring system required
- **Never-miss test:** **Breast USS** when abscess is suspected – omission leads to delayed drainage and ongoing sepsis.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Start empiric antibiotics if fever or systemic symptoms present (see Drugs & Doses)
  - Advise to continue breastfeeding or expressing from both breasts (including affected side) – do not stop feeding
  - Warm compress or gentle massage before feeds to promote outflow
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g QDS or ibuprofen 400mg TDS (if no contraindications)
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - If admitted (sepsis, abscess, high NEWS2): IV antibiotics, IV fluids (0.9% sodium chloride 500ml–1L stat, then [check local protocol] for rate)
  - If abscess confirmed: arrange aspiration (USS-guided) or incision & drainage – refer to breast surgery team
  - Monitor temperature, pain, and resolution of erythema
  - Support lactation: lactation consultant referral
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete 10–14 day course of antibiotics (oral switch when afebrile for 24 h)
  - Continue breastfeeding on both sides; if infant refuses affected breast, hand express and feed by cup
  - Consider probiotic (Lactobacillus) for recurrent mastitis – limited evidence but low risk
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 125 ml/h (adjust for shock)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (unless low risk or contraindicated)
  - Diet: normal diet, encourage oral intake (no NBM)
  - Observations: 4-hourly (more frequent if septic – 1–2 hourly)
  - Breastfeed/pump as usual (involve midwifery/neonatal team)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Flucloxacillin** 500 mg QDS PO (mild–moderate) OR 1 g QDS IV (severe) for 10–14 days – covers *Staph aureus*.
  - If penicillin allergy (non-anaphylaxis): **Cefalexin** 500 mg QDS PO (or 1 g IV) – cross-reactivity low.
  - If penicillin anaphylaxis: **Clindamycin** 300–450 mg QDS PO (or 600 mg IV QDS) – covers *Staph* and *Strep*.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Co-amoxiclav** 625 mg TDS PO (if cephalosporin allergy or mixed infection) – note: may cause diarrhoea in infant.
  - MRSA risk (known colonisation, recurrent): **Vancomycin** IV (dose per protocol) – seek microbiology advice.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Flucloxacillin + warfarin – enhanced anticoagulation (monitor INR)
  - Clindamycin – higher risk of *C. difficile* infection
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Flucloxacillin: avoid higher doses (e.g. >4 g/day IV) in hepatic impairment – risk of jaundice (especially elderly)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Prescribing flucloxacillin without confirming penicillin allergy status** – can cause anaphylaxis. Always ask: “Have you ever had a reaction to penicillin?”
  - **Omitting analgesia** – pain inhibits milk let-down; ensure paracetamol/ibuprofen prescribed.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Breast abscess (requires drainage)
  - Septic shock
  - Recurrent mastitis (if incomplete treatment or underlying blocked duct)
- **Chronic:**
  - Milk fistula (after incision & drainage – rare)
  - Scarring or fibrosis
  - Inflammatory breast cancer mimic – delayed diagnosis if not considered

### Prognosis
- Excellent with early antibiotics – >90% resolve without abscess. Abscess requires drainage but breastfeeding can continue. Recurrence rate ~10–20% if unresolved risk factors (poor latch, nipple damage). No increase in mortality.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always advise continued breastfeeding/expressing** – stopping worsens engorgement and infection risk. If infant refuses, pump and discard (can return to breast after treatment).
- **Consider breast milk culture** for recurrent or antibiotic-resistant cases – send mid-stream milk (after cleaning nipple) to microbiology.
- **Examine both breasts and check for nipple fissures/GDH (group B streptococcus) in pregnancy** – if fissure present, apply mupirocin 2% ointment TDS for 5 days (if crusts/erythema).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT prescribe co-amoxiclav as first-line without checking local guidelines** – may cause diarrhoea in infant; flucloxacillin is preferred.
- **Do NOT delay abscess drainage** – waiting beyond 48 h of medical therapy increases risk of rupture, fistula, and prolonged sepsis.
- **Do NOT dismiss inflammatory breast cancer in a non-lactating woman with persistent erythema** – always refer to urgent breast clinic if no response to 2 weeks of antibiotics.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Fever >38.5°C after 48 h of antibiotics
  - Worsening pain or swelling despite treatment
  - Development of a new, fluctuant lump
  - Inability to breastfeed/express (increasing breast fullness)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP review in 48–72 h to assess response
  - If recurrent mastitis (>2 episodes): refer to breast surgeon or lactation consultant
  - If abscess drained: review by breast surgery team in 1–2 weeks

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially respiratory rate >24, SpO2 <92%, systolic BP <90 mmHg)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L despite fluid resuscitation
  - GCS <15 or confusion
  - Abscess not amenable to bedside drainage (requires USS-guided aspiration or I&D in theatre)
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - Septic shock persisting after 1 L fluid bolus
  - Rising lactate (>4 mmol/L)
  - Need for vasopressors or respiratory support
